Category: --- CONSTRUCTION PERMITS: the primary jurisdictional authorizations required to build ---

Description: Primary authorization from the local building department to begin construction. Lifecycle: Application → Plan Review → Approval → Issuance → Active → Final Inspection → Closed. Specifies scope of permitted work, applicable building codes, occupancy classification, construction type, and conditions of approval. Required before any structural or building system work begins. Issued by the Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ).
Industry Standard: IBC (International Building Code); local building codes; IECC (energy code); ADA/ICC A117.1 (accessibility)
Common Values: New Construction, Addition, Tenant Improvement, Shell & Core, Foundation Only, Partial Permit (early start)
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals (Accela / Citizenserve / EnerGov); architect offices; Excel; email; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit tracking module; permits tracked as uploaded documents or action plan items
Benchmarking Use: Permit approval cycle time by jurisdiction and project type reveals regulatory efficiency and planning requirements
Notes: Critical path item — construction cannot legally start without an issued building permit. Most jurisdictions allow early-start or partial permits for foundations while full permit is pending. The permit set of drawings is the code-reviewed approved construction document set.

Description: Authorization to demolish or remove existing structures. Lifecycle: Application → Hazmat Survey → Review → Approval → Issuance → Active → Completion Notification. Requires documentation of hazardous material surveys (asbestos / lead / PCBs) and abatement plans before issuance. Separate from building permit in most jurisdictions.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart T (Demolition); EPA NESHAP (asbestos); local demolition ordinances
Common Values: Full Demolition, Partial/Selective Demolition, Interior Demolition, Soft Strip
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; environmental consulting firms; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit tracking; demolition scope tracked in daily logs and action plans
Benchmarking Use: Demolition permit timelines by jurisdiction indicate hazmat survey requirements and regulatory complexity
Notes: Asbestos survey is typically a prerequisite — jurisdictions won't issue demo permits without a completed survey. NESHAP notification required 10 working days before demolition of structures with regulated asbestos.

Description: Authorization for modifications to existing structures — alterations / repairs / changes of use. Triggers when work exceeds substantial improvement thresholds defined by the jurisdiction (often 50% of building value). May trigger full code compliance for the entire building vs. only the work area depending on scope classification. Decision authority: building official determines scope classification.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 34 (Existing Buildings); IEBC (International Existing Building Code); local amendments
Common Values: Alteration Level 1/2/3, Change of Occupancy, Repair, Addition, Substantial Improvement
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; code analysis reports; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit tracking; renovation scope defined in project setup and specifications
Benchmarking Use: Renovation permit complexity by alteration level and building age highlights code compliance cost drivers
Notes: Often more complex than new construction — requires code analysis of the existing building. The IEBC provides prescriptive (3 alteration levels) and performance paths. Historic buildings may have additional preservation requirements.

Description: Authorization for temporary construction facilities — scaffolding / shoring / formwork / temporary enclosures / construction hoists. Required for structures affecting public safety or right-of-way. Lifecycle: Application → Engineering Review → Issuance → Active → Inspection → Removal → Closeout. Typically requires PE-stamped drawings for scaffolding over certain heights.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart L (Scaffolds); ANSI/ASSE A10.8 (scaffolding safety); local codes
Common Values: Scaffold, Sidewalk Shed, Construction Fence, Tower Crane Foundation, Shoring, Temporary Enclosure, Construction Hoist
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; scaffold engineering firms; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit tracking; tracked via action plans or daily log entries
Benchmarking Use: Temporary structure permit duration by type reveals project-specific overhead costs and site logistics complexity
Notes: In urban environments (NYC / Chicago / SF) sidewalk shed and scaffold permits are major cost drivers with annual renewal requirements. Tower crane permits may require FAA notification. Many temporary structure permits have time limits requiring renewal.

Description: Separate jurisdictional permits required for specific building system work — electrical / plumbing / mechanical / fire protection / elevator. Each trade permit authorizes work by licensed contractors in that discipline and triggers trade-specific rough-in and final inspections. Some jurisdictions issue trade permits as part of the building permit; others require separate applications per trade.
Industry Standard: NEC (National Electrical Code); UPC/IPC (Plumbing Code); UMC/IMC (Mechanical Code); NFPA 13/14/72 (Fire Protection); ASME A17.1 (Elevators)
Common Values: Electrical, Plumbing, Mechanical/HVAC, Fire Protection (sprinkler), Fire Alarm, Elevator, Gas, Low Voltage
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; trade contractor offices; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit tracking; trade work tracked through daily logs and field inspections
Benchmarking Use: Trade permit count and cycle time by project type indicate system complexity and jurisdictional requirements
Notes: Trade permits are typically pulled by the responsible subcontractor (electrician pulls electrical permit) not the GC. Some jurisdictions combine fire sprinkler and fire alarm into one fire permit; others separate them. Elevator permits often involve a separate state agency.

Category: --- SITE & ENVIRONMENTAL PERMITS: regulatory authorizations for site disturbance and environmental protection ---

Description: Regulatory authorizations for work affecting air quality / water resources / wetlands / hazardous materials. Issued by federal (EPA) / state / and local environmental agencies. Lifecycle: Environmental Assessment → Application → Agency Review → Issuance → Compliance Monitoring → Closeout. May require Environmental Impact Statements (EIS) or Assessments (EA) for large or sensitive projects.
Industry Standard: Clean Air Act; Clean Water Act (Section 404); NEPA; RCRA (hazmat); state environmental regulations
Common Values: Air Quality Permit, Wetlands Permit (Section 404), Hazardous Materials Permit, Noise Variance, Dust Control Permit, Dewatering Permit
Source Systems: EPA portals; state environmental agencies; environmental consulting firms; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — environmental notes captured in daily logs; compliance documents uploadable but not structured for permit tracking
Benchmarking Use: Environmental permit count and review duration by project type and location indicate regulatory complexity and potential delays
Notes: Often the longest-lead regulatory item — wetlands permits can take 6-18 months. Projects near sensitive receptors (schools / hospitals / waterways) face stricter requirements. NEPA applies only to federally funded or permitted projects.

Description: Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan and associated permit required for sites disturbing one or more acres. Mandates erosion and sediment control (ESC) best management practices (BMPs). Lifecycle: NOI Filing → SWPPP Preparation → Permit Coverage → Active Monitoring → NOT (Notice of Termination). Requires weekly inspections and post-rain-event inspections with corrective actions for BMP deficiencies.
Industry Standard: Clean Water Act Section 402; EPA Construction General Permit (CGP); state NPDES permits
Common Values: Notice of Intent (NOI), Notice of Termination (NOT), BMP Inspection, Corrective Action, Turbidity Monitoring
Source Systems: EPA eNOI system; state NPDES portals; environmental consultants; daily logs; Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — BMP inspections may use inspection checklists; SWPPP documents uploaded as attachments; no structured stormwater tracking
Benchmarking Use: SWPPP violation frequency by project type and region indicates site management effectiveness and regulatory exposure
Notes: One of the most common regulatory violations on construction sites — EPA fines can exceed $50K/day. Many states have stricter requirements than federal CGP. GC is typically the permit holder but shares responsibility with the owner.

Description: Authorization for construction activities within the public right-of-way — lane closures / sidewalk closures / utility cuts / material staging / crane operations over public streets. Issued by local transportation or public works departments. Often time-restricted (nighttime work only / no closures during rush hour) with traffic control plan requirements.
Industry Standard: MUTCD (Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices); local DOT requirements; utility coordination standards
Common Values: Lane Closure, Sidewalk Closure, Street Closure, Utility Cut, Crane Swing Over ROW, Material Staging, Dumpster Placement
Source Systems: Municipal DOT portals; traffic engineering firms; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native ROW permit tracking; may be tracked via action plans or project-level documents
Benchmarking Use: ROW permit restrictions by jurisdiction and project location affect construction logistics cost and schedule
Notes: Urban projects may require multiple ROW permits throughout construction — each crane pick / concrete pour / or steel delivery that affects traffic may need separate authorization.

Description: Authorization to connect to or modify public utility infrastructure — water / sanitary sewer / storm sewer / gas / electric / telecommunications. Each utility provider has separate application processes and design review requirements. Lifecycle: Application → Design Review → Fee Payment → Scheduling → Connection → Inspection → Activation. Often on the critical path for project completion and occupancy.
Industry Standard: Utility-specific standards (AWWA for water / NESC for electric); local utility design standards; one-call/811 requirements
Common Values: Water Main Tap, Sewer Connection, Storm Drain Connection, Gas Service, Electrical Service, Telecom/Fiber
Source Systems: Utility company portals; municipal engineering departments; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native utility permit tracking; utility coordination tracked in daily logs and meeting minutes
Benchmarking Use: Utility connection lead time by utility type and provider reveals scheduling risk and infrastructure planning requirements
Notes: Utility connections require coordination with utility companies on THEIR schedules — the GC has limited control over timing. Temporary construction utilities (temp power / temp water) require separate arrangements. Utility conflicts during excavation are a major rework trigger.

Category: --- ZONING & LAND USE: land use authorizations and exceptions from local planning authorities ---

Description: Land use authorization confirming that the proposed project complies with local zoning ordinances — permitted uses / setbacks / height limits / lot coverage / floor area ratio (FAR) / parking requirements / density. Typically required before building permit application. Lifecycle: Zoning Review → Staff Report → Public Hearing (if required) → Approval / Denial. Decision authority: zoning administrator or planning commission.
Industry Standard: Local zoning ordinances; state enabling legislation; form-based codes
Common Values: By-Right (permitted use), Conditional Use Permit (CUP), Special Use Permit (SUP), Site Plan Approval, Planned Unit Development (PUD)
Source Systems: Municipal planning/zoning portals; land use attorneys; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — zoning is a preconstruction/entitlement process outside construction PM software scope
Benchmarking Use: Zoning approval timeline by jurisdiction and entitlement type indicates project feasibility risk
Notes: Typically an owner/developer responsibility handled during preconstruction not by the GC. However zoning conditions (hours of operation / noise limits / truck routes) directly constrain construction operations. Rezoning or variances can add 3-12+ months to preconstruction.

Description: Formal relief from specific zoning requirements when strict compliance creates undue hardship. Lifecycle: Application → Staff Review → Public Hearing → Board Decision → Appeal Period. Requires demonstration of hardship (not self-created) and that the variance won't adversely affect surrounding properties. Decision authority: Board of Zoning Appeals or equivalent.
Industry Standard: State zoning enabling acts; local board of appeals procedures
Common Values: Area Variance (setback / height / lot coverage), Use Variance, Special Exception, Nonconforming Use
Source Systems: Municipal zoning board records; land use attorneys; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — zoning variance process is outside construction PM software scope; conditions may appear in project specifications
Benchmarking Use: Variance frequency by project type and jurisdiction indicates code flexibility and design constraint patterns
Notes: Use variances (allowing a prohibited use) are much harder to obtain than area variances (modifying dimensional requirements). Approved variances may include conditions that constrain construction (e.g. construction hours / screening requirements).

Category: --- PLAN REVIEW & REGULATORY APPROVALS: formal review and approval by regulatory agencies before construction ---

Description: Formal review of construction documents by the building department to verify code compliance before permit issuance. Covers structural / life safety / accessibility / energy / and building code requirements. Lifecycle: Submittal → Initial Review → Comments Issued → Resubmittal → Approval → Permit Issuance. Decision authority: plans examiner and building official. Multiple review cycles are common — first review often returns 50-200+ comments.
Industry Standard: IBC; IRC; IECC; ADA/ICC A117.1; ASCE 7 (structural loads); local amendments
Common Values: First Review, Second Review, Third Review, Expedited Review, Deferred Submittal, Phased Review
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals (Accela / Citizenserve / EnerGov); third-party plan review firms; email; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — review status may be tracked via submittals workflow or action plans; no native plan review tracking
Benchmarking Use: Plan review cycle count and duration by jurisdiction indicate design document quality and regulatory throughput
Notes: Many jurisdictions now accept or require electronic plan review (EPR). Third-party plan review firms supplement building department staff. Deferred submittals (structural connections / curtain wall shop drawings) allow permit issuance before all design is complete.

Description: Fire and life safety plan review and field approval by the fire marshal or fire prevention bureau. Covers fire sprinkler design / fire alarm systems / means of egress / fire-rated assemblies / hazardous materials storage / and fire access. Often a parallel review track to the building department. Lifecycle: Submittal → Review → Comments → Resubmittal → Approval → Field Inspections → Final Sign-Off.
Industry Standard: NFPA 1 (Fire Code); NFPA 13/13R/13D (Sprinklers); NFPA 14 (Standpipes); NFPA 72 (Fire Alarm); NFPA 101 (Life Safety Code); IFC
Common Values: Fire Sprinkler Review, Fire Alarm Review, Means of Egress Review, Hazmat Review, Fire Access Review, Underground Fire Line
Source Systems: Fire department plan review portals; fire protection engineering firms; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — fire protection field checks may use the inspections module; plan review status tracked as documents
Benchmarking Use: Fire marshal review duration and rejection rate by system type reveal design coordination quality
Notes: Fire marshal and building department reviews are often independent — separate agencies with separate timelines. Fire sprinkler shop drawings are the most common deferred submittal often reviewed directly by the fire marshal.

Description: Regulatory approval from the health department for projects involving food service / healthcare / pools / laboratories / or other health-regulated occupancies. Reviews focus on sanitation / food safety / infection control / ventilation / and hazardous material handling. Required in addition to building department approval. Decision authority: health officer or environmental health specialist.
Industry Standard: FDA Food Code; CMS (healthcare); state health codes; local health department regulations
Common Values: Food Service Review, Healthcare Plan Review, Pool/Spa Permit, Laboratory Safety Review, Child Care Facility Review
Source Systems: Health department portals; healthcare design consultants; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native health department tracking; health-specific field checks may use the inspections module
Benchmarking Use: Health department approval timeline by facility type indicates design complexity and specialized compliance requirements
Notes: Healthcare projects face the most complex health department requirements — infection control risk assessments (ICRA) / negative pressure room verification / medical gas system testing. Restaurant/food service approvals can delay tenant improvement completion.

Category: --- REGULATORY INSPECTIONS: field inspections by authorities and independent testing agencies ---

Description: Field inspections performed by the Authority Having Jurisdiction (building department) at required construction milestones. Verifies that work in place complies with approved plans and applicable codes. Lifecycle: Request → Scheduling → Inspection → Pass / Fail / Conditional. Typical sequence: foundation / slab / rough framing / rough MEP / insulation / drywall / final. Failed inspections require correction and re-inspection.
Industry Standard: IBC Section 110 (Required Inspections); local inspection requirements; state-specific inspection schedules
Common Values: Foundation, Slab/Deck, Rough Framing, Rough Electrical, Rough Plumbing, Rough Mechanical, Insulation, Final, Reinspection
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; inspection scheduling apps; Procore; daily logs; Excel
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections and inspection items tables can model AHJ inspections with configurable checklists and scheduling; inspection scheduling for recurring inspections
Benchmarking Use: AHJ inspection first-pass rate by trade and project type reveals construction quality and code compliance effectiveness
Notes: The GC coordinates AHJ inspections — calling at the right time to avoid delays. A failed inspection can hold up subsequent work. Some jurisdictions have 24-48 hour scheduling lead times. Best practice: superintendent walks the work before calling for inspection (pre-inspection).

Description: Code-required inspections performed by independent certified special inspectors (not the building department) for critical structural and life-safety work. Required where deficiencies would be concealed and could create structural failure. The special inspector is hired by the owner (not the contractor) and reports to the building official. Lifecycle: Statement of Special Inspections → Inspector Qualification → Ongoing Inspections → Reports → Final Letter.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 17 (Special Inspections and Tests); ACI 318 (Concrete); AWS D1.1 (Structural Welding); AISC 341/360 (Steel)
Common Values: Structural Steel (bolting/welding), Concrete Placement, Reinforcing Steel, Masonry, Fireproofing, Spray-Applied Fire-Resistive Materials, Soil/Earthwork, Pile Driving, Post-Installed Anchors
Source Systems: Special inspection agencies; testing labs; Procore; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspections module can track special inspection checklists; results documented as attachments; no structured test data
Benchmarking Use: Special inspection deficiency rate by material type and trade contractor reveals workmanship quality
Notes: The Statement of Special Inspections (SSI) from the design team lists all required special inspections — it's the compliance roadmap. Special inspectors must be certified (ICC / AWS / ACI). Continuous vs. periodic requirements vary by code and material. Building official won't approve CO without final special inspection letters.

Description: Independent materials testing and quality assurance performed by certified testing laboratories. Verifies that construction materials and installed systems meet specifications and code requirements. Common tests: concrete cylinder breaks / soil compaction (Proctor) / steel tension testing / weld inspection / air and water infiltration / duct leakage. Results documented in formal lab reports.
Industry Standard: ASTM standards (C39 concrete / D1557 soil / E1105 water infiltration); ACI 301; AASHTO; local testing requirements
Common Values: Concrete Cylinder Break, Soil Compaction (nuclear density), Steel Tensile Test, Weld Inspection (UT/MT/PT), Air Infiltration, Water Test, Duct Leakage, Electrical Grounding
Source Systems: Testing laboratories; geotechnical firms; Procore (as document attachments); Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — test reports uploaded as documents or attachments; no structured test result data (pass/fail values / PSI / compaction percentages)
Benchmarking Use: Test failure rate by material type and supplier/trade reveals material quality and workmanship consistency
Notes: Concrete cylinder breaks are the highest-volume test on most projects — 7-day and 28-day breaks are standard. Failed tests can require structural remediation (core drilling / load testing) or removal and replacement. Testing labs are coordinated by GC but hired by owner to maintain independence.

Category: --- CERTIFICATES & OCCUPANCY: final regulatory sign-offs authorizing building use and occupancy ---

Description: Final authorization from the building department to occupy and use a completed building for its permitted purpose. Confirms all code requirements met / all required inspections passed / all special inspection letters received. Lifecycle: Application → Final Inspections → Documentation Review → Issuance. The CO is the ultimate regulatory deliverable — unlocks the owner's ability to occupy / lease / or operate the facility.
Industry Standard: IBC Section 111 (Certificate of Occupancy); local certificate requirements
Common Values: Full CO, Partial CO (by floor or area), Core & Shell CO, Amended CO (change of use)
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; building department offices; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native CO tracking; status typically tracked in project meetings and closeout checklists
Benchmarking Use: Time from substantial completion to CO by jurisdiction and project type reveals closeout efficiency and regulatory timeline risk
Notes: Often the contractual milestone triggering final payment / retainage release / warranty period start. Partial COs allow phased occupancy but may have conditions. Operating without a CO is illegal in most jurisdictions and can result in vacate orders.

Description: Conditional occupancy approval when a building is safe to occupy but has outstanding items preventing full CO. Specifies conditions and deadline for completing remaining work. Lifecycle: Application → Review → Issuance (with conditions) → Renewal/Extension → Conversion to Full CO. Carries a list of open items (fire alarm completion / punch list / landscaping) and typically expires in 90-180 days.
Industry Standard: IBC Section 111.3 (Temporary Occupancy); local TCO procedures
Common Values: 90-Day TCO, 120-Day TCO, 180-Day TCO, Renewed TCO, Conditional TCO
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; building department offices; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native TCO tracking; conditions typically managed through punch lists and action plans
Benchmarking Use: TCO-to-CO conversion time reveals project closeout effectiveness and outstanding item resolution speed
Notes: Common on large commercial projects where full completion is impractical by move-in date. Multiple TCO renewals are a red flag for closeout problems. Some jurisdictions (notably NYC) have strict renewal limits and escalating fees. GC remains responsible for open TCO items.

Description: Regulatory sign-off confirming that construction work is complete and compliant — used for projects without occupancy (site work / infrastructure / demolition) or for trade-specific scope completion. Lifecycle: Application → Final Inspection → Documentation Review → Issuance. Distinct from a CO which authorizes occupancy.
Industry Standard: IBC Section 111.1; local building department procedures; DOT requirements for public infrastructure
Common Values: Building Completion, Site Work Completion, Infrastructure Completion, Demolition Completion
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; building department offices; paper
Procore Coverage: None — no native tracking; completion tracked through project closeout processes
Benchmarking Use: Time from last inspection to certificate issuance reveals closeout documentation quality
Notes: For public infrastructure projects (roads / bridges / utilities) the Certificate of Completion is the primary regulatory closeout instrument. Triggers warranty period and may require as-built documentation / operations manuals / spare parts delivery.

Description: Written confirmation from a regulatory agency / design professional / or special inspector that specific work or systems comply with applicable codes and standards. Lifecycle: Inspection/Testing → Report → Letter Issuance. Each letter covers a specific scope. The building department typically requires a complete set of compliance letters before issuing a CO.
Industry Standard: IBC Chapter 17 (Special Inspection letters); ASME A17.1 (Elevator Acceptance); ASHRAE 90.1 (Energy); state-specific requirements
Common Values: Structural Compliance Letter, Fire Protection Sign-Off, Elevator Acceptance, Energy Compliance Certificate, Accessibility Compliance, Electrical Sign-Off, Plumbing Sign-Off
Source Systems: Special inspection agencies; design professionals; trade inspectors; paper; email
Procore Coverage: None — no native compliance letter tracking; letters uploaded as closeout documents
Benchmarking Use: Missing compliance letter count during closeout indicates documentation coordination gaps
Notes: Compliance letters are the connective tissue of closeout — each regulatory agency requires its own sign-off before releasing hold on the CO. A single missing letter can delay occupancy. The Statement of Special Inspections from design phase defines which letters will be required.

Category: --- SAFETY & LABOR COMPLIANCE: OSHA requirements and workforce regulatory obligations ---

Description: Required notifications to OSHA and activity-specific safety permits for hazardous construction operations. Includes crane assembly/disassembly notifications / confined space entry permits / excavation permits (depths over 20 feet in some states) / lead and asbestos abatement notifications / and blasting permits. Lifecycle: Notification/Application → Review → Issuance → Active → Closeout.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 (Construction Safety); OSHA 29 CFR 1910.146 (Confined Space); state OSHA plans; EPA (asbestos/lead)
Common Values: Crane Notification, Confined Space Entry Permit, Excavation Permit, Hot Work Permit, Asbestos Notification, Lead Notification, Blasting Permit
Source Systems: OSHA portals; state OSHA agencies; Procore (incident and safety observation tracking); safety management software; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — OSHA-recordable incidents tracked via incident tables; safety observations capture hazards; no native activity permit tracking
Benchmarking Use: OSHA notification frequency by project type indicates hazardous activity exposure and safety program maturity
Notes: Hot work permits and confined space entry permits are managed internally by the GC's safety department not filed with OSHA. Crane notifications may require 24-48 hour advance notice. OSHA can inspect any construction site without notice.

Description: Project-specific safety documentation identifying hazards / control measures / emergency procedures / and safety responsibilities. May include fall protection plan / hazard communication plan / crane lift plans / traffic control plans / and fire prevention plan. Required by OSHA and often by the owner's contract. Lifecycle: Development → Submission → Owner/CM Review → Approval → Active → Updates as Conditions Change.
Industry Standard: OSHA 29 CFR 1926 Subpart C (General Safety); ANSI/ASSP Z359 (Fall Protection); owner/CM-specific requirements
Common Values: Site-Specific Safety Plan (SSSP), Fall Protection Plan, Crane Lift Plan, Traffic Control Plan, Hazard Communication Plan, Fire Prevention Plan, Emergency Action Plan
Source Systems: Safety management software (Procore / iAuditor / SafetyCulture); Excel/Word; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — safety plans uploaded as documents; safety observations and field inspections provide ongoing monitoring; no structured safety plan management
Benchmarking Use: Safety plan completeness and update frequency indicate safety program maturity and proactive risk management
Notes: A living document that should be updated as conditions change (new trades / new hazards / crane operations). Many owners/CMs require safety plan approval before GC mobilization. Subcontractors may be required to submit their own safety plans.

Description: Requirements for workforce compensation / documentation / and equal opportunity — particularly on public and federally funded projects. Includes prevailing wage determinations / certified payroll reporting / apprenticeship ratios / and diversity goals (DBE / MBE / WBE / Section 3). Lifecycle: Wage Determination → Compliance Plan → Ongoing Reporting → Audits → Closeout Certification.
Industry Standard: Davis-Bacon Act (federal); state prevailing wage laws; Executive Order 11246 (EEO); local workforce requirements
Common Values: Prevailing Wage, Certified Payroll, Apprenticeship Ratio, DBE/MBE/WBE Goals, Section 3, Project Labor Agreement (PLA), Local Hire Requirements
Source Systems: LCPtracker; Elations; B2GNow; state labor department portals; payroll systems; Excel
Procore Coverage: None — no native certified payroll or labor compliance tracking; workforce data in daily logs is activity-based not compliance-based
Benchmarking Use: Labor compliance violation rate and workforce diversity metrics by project type reveal regulatory risk exposure
Notes: Prevailing wage applies to most public construction in the US. Violations can result in back pay / debarment from public work / contract termination. GC collects and submits certified payroll from all subcontractors. Private commercial projects generally don't have prevailing wage unless receiving public funding.

Category: --- PERMIT LIFECYCLE & TRACKING: the process of applying for, managing, and closing out permits ---

Description: The formal request to a regulatory agency for a permit / approval / or certificate. Includes project information / scope of work / applicable codes / required documents (plans / calculations / surveys) / and fees. Each agency has its own format and requirements. Lifecycle: Preparation → Submission → Completeness Check → Accepted for Review → Under Review → Comments → Resubmission → Approval.
Industry Standard: Local building department application requirements; state-specific forms; online filing standards
Common Values: Building Permit Application, Trade Permit Application, Environmental Permit Application, ROW Permit Application, Zoning Application
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals (Accela / Citizenserve / EnerGov / Amanda / Tyler); architect/engineer offices; Excel; paper
Procore Coverage: Low — permit applications may be tracked via action plans or submittals workflow; no native permit application tracking
Benchmarking Use: Permit application completeness rate (first submission acceptance) indicates design document quality and regulatory experience
Notes: Incomplete applications are the #1 cause of permit delays — jurisdictions reject submissions missing required documents / calculations / or fees. Permit expediting firms specialize in navigating complex municipal processes.

Description: The current state of a permit in its lifecycle — from application through issuance / active construction / and final closeout. Tracking status across all required permits is a critical path management activity. A single expired or missing permit can halt construction and trigger stop-work orders. Decision authority: building official / fire marshal / or other AHJ depending on permit type.
Industry Standard: No universal standard — each jurisdiction defines its own status terminology
Common Values: Applied, Under Review, Comments Issued, Approved, Issued, Active, Expired, Renewed, Suspended, Revoked, Closed, Finaled
Source Systems: Municipal permit portals; Excel/Google Sheets trackers; Procore (as document status); email
Procore Coverage: Low — no native permit status field; may be tracked through custom fields / action plans / or document annotations
Benchmarking Use: Permit status aging (days in each state) by jurisdiction reveals regulatory bottlenecks and resource planning needs
Notes: Most GCs maintain a permit tracking log — a spreadsheet listing all required permits / status / responsible party / and dates. Prime candidate for structured data capture. Expired permits require renewal which may trigger re-inspection.

Description: The process of requesting / scheduling / and coordinating regulatory inspections with the AHJ / special inspectors / and testing labs. Requires sequencing inspections to match construction progress — calling at the right time to avoid delays while not covering up work before it's inspected. Lifecycle: Request → Schedule → Inspect → Pass/Fail → Reinspect (if failed) → Document.
Industry Standard: IBC Section 110; local inspection scheduling procedures; ASTM testing schedules
Common Values: 24-Hour Request, 48-Hour Request, Same-Day, Online Scheduling, Phone Request, Batch Scheduling
Source Systems: Municipal inspection scheduling portals; Procore; phone/email; daily logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — inspection scheduling tables support recurring and one-time scheduling; inspections module can coordinate internal and regulatory inspections
Benchmarking Use: Inspection lead time and coordination efficiency reveal project team organizational capability
Notes: Failed inspections are a schedule killer — correction work and reinspection often lose 2-5+ days. Special inspections for structural work must be scheduled to be present DURING the work (continuous inspection for welding / concrete placement).

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: derived KPIs for regulatory and permit performance ---

Description: Elapsed time from permit application submission to permit issuance. Calculation: issuance date minus application date / optionally excluding resubmission periods. Segmented by permit type (building / trade / environmental) and jurisdiction. Longer cycle times indicate regulatory complexity / application quality issues / or agency resource constraints.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; AACE; industry benchmarking studies
Common Values: 2-4 weeks (simple trade permits), 4-12 weeks (building permits), 3-18 months (environmental/zoning)
Source Systems: Permit tracking logs; municipal portals (Accela / Citizenserve); Excel; Procore (if tracked via action plans)
Procore Coverage: Low — derivable if permit milestones tracked via action plans or custom workflows; no native permit date tracking
Benchmarking Use: Permit cycle time by jurisdiction / permit type / and project type reveals regulatory planning requirements for future projects
Notes: One of the most requested preconstruction metrics — owners want to know how long permits will take. Experienced teams budget for 2-3 review cycles. Expedited review (for additional fees) available in many jurisdictions.

Description: Percentage of regulatory inspections (AHJ and special) that pass on the first attempt. Calculation: passed inspections / total inspections × 100. A high first-pass rate indicates quality construction / proper sequencing / and effective pre-inspection practices. Segmented by inspection type / trade / and inspector.
Industry Standard: Industry benchmark: 85-95% first-pass rate for well-managed projects
Common Values: 85-95% (well-managed), 70-84% (average), <70% (quality concerns)
Source Systems: Municipal inspection records; Procore (inspections module); special inspection agencies; daily logs
Procore Coverage: Medium — derivable from inspections and inspection items tables where pass/fail results are recorded
Benchmarking Use: Inspection pass rate by trade and inspection type identifies quality weak points and training needs
Notes: Failed inspections should be tracked by root cause — premature call (work not ready) vs. workmanship deficiency vs. code interpretation disagreement. The distinction drives different corrective actions.

Description: Total calendar days of construction delay caused by regulatory actions — stop-work orders / permit delays / failed inspections requiring correction / expired permits. Calculation: sum of days from hold issuance to resolution for all regulatory events. A leading indicator of regulatory risk exposure and compliance management effectiveness.
Industry Standard: AACE delay classification; scheduling impact analysis standards
Common Values: 0-5 days (well-managed), 5-20 days (average), 20+ days (regulatory risk)
Source Systems: Permit tracking logs; daily logs (delay entries); schedule impact records; Procore (delay log entries); Excel
Procore Coverage: Low — daily log delay entries capture delay events that may include regulatory holds; no native stop-work order tracking
Benchmarking Use: Regulatory hold duration by project type and jurisdiction quantifies regulatory risk for project planning
Notes: Stop-work orders are the most severe regulatory action — they halt all construction and can take days to weeks to resolve. Causes include expired permits / work without permits / safety violations / and environmental violations.

Description: Average elapsed days that permits remain in open/active status. Calculation: sum of (current date or closure date minus issuance date) / total permits. Tracks whether permits are being closed out in a timely manner. Open permits at project completion indicate incomplete final inspections or documentation gaps.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; project-specific tracking
Common Values: 30-180 days (typical active period), 180+ days (aging concern), open at closeout (red flag)
Source Systems: Permit tracking logs; municipal portals; Excel
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit aging tracking; requires external permit management
Benchmarking Use: Open permit count at substantial completion reveals closeout risk and regulatory documentation effectiveness
Notes: Open permits at project completion are a common problem — the GC moves on and forgets to close permits. Open permits can prevent CO issuance and create liability for the owner. Some jurisdictions automatically expire permits after inactivity.

Description: Percentage of required regulatory and compliance documents received and verified. Calculation: received documents / total required documents × 100. Includes insurance certificates / special inspection letters / compliance letters / as-built certifications / test reports. A closeout readiness indicator.
Industry Standard: Owner/CM contract requirements; IBC documentation requirements
Common Values: 100% (closeout ready), 90-99% (near complete), <90% (significant gaps)
Source Systems: Procore (contract compliance documents module); document management systems; Excel; paper files
Procore Coverage: Medium — contract compliance documents table tracks required documents and their status; attachments support upload
Benchmarking Use: Compliance document completeness trajectory over project duration reveals documentation management discipline
Notes: A structured compliance document checklist at project kickoff (listing all required letters / certificates / reports) is best practice. Last-minute document chasing is one of the most frustrating closeout activities.

Description: Percentage of permits that require renewal or extension during the project — indicating original validity period was insufficient. Calculation: renewed permits / total permits × 100. High renewal rates indicate schedule overruns / permit validity misalignment / or phasing issues.
Industry Standard: No universal standard; jurisdiction-specific validity periods (6 months to 2 years)
Common Values: 0-5% (on-schedule), 5-15% (schedule pressure), 15%+ (significant delays)
Source Systems: Permit tracking logs; municipal portals; Excel
Procore Coverage: None — no native permit renewal tracking
Benchmarking Use: Permit renewal rate by project type correlates with schedule performance and indicates planning accuracy
Notes: Permit renewal fees can be significant on large projects. Some jurisdictions require re-inspection or updated plans for renewal. Automatic expiration (typically 180 days of inactivity) is the most common renewal trigger.
